one_line_takeaway: A sub-25-line modification of Anthropic's official weather-server example, plus a free webhook, was enough to make Claude Desktop read and exfiltrate a real bank balance from a co-installed banking MCP server, showing that MCP's composability plus implicit cross-server trust is a low-barrier exfiltration vector even when consent prompts fire.
key_finding: The attack succeeded: the victim's real Monzo balance of £1,469.36, plus location and timestamp, was captured in JSON via HTTP POST at the attacker's webhook.site dashboard. Total setup time was under 2 hours; the webhook endpoint was generated in ~1 second with no registration; all components were free, public, or from official docs. The paper attributes exploitability to three systemic weaknesses: (1) protocol permissiveness (per-tool permission prompts are client-optional, only 'recommended' in MCP docs, not protocol-enforced, so a silent client would enable automated harvesting); (2) composability without security boundaries (any installed server can trigger tool calls on any other via the agent, and users cannot designate servers as sensitive/isolated); and (3) ecosystem velocity (unvetted servers proliferate on GitHub and community directories).
core_contribution: A proof-of-concept demonstration that the Model Context Protocol (MCP) trust model permits trivial cross-server data exfiltration. The authors show that a malicious MCP server, built by adding fewer than 25 lines to Anthropic's official 'Hello Weather' example, can induce an AI agent (Claude Desktop) to discover a co-installed legitimate banking server, read the user's real bank balance, and exfiltrate it to a free public webhook. The paper's three stated contributions are: (1) a concrete cross-server MCP attack that exfiltrates real financial data; (2) quantification of the minimal skills/resources required; and (3) proposed immediate mitigations plus protocol-level interventions. The central insight is that individual servers can each look benign while their composition creates an unanticipated cross-server attack surface mediated by the agent.
threat_model: Adversary is an unsophisticated author of a single malicious MCP server (undergraduate-level Python, no infrastructure, no funds, no authentication bypass, no reverse engineering). The victim has voluntarily installed both the malicious server (disguised as a weather tool) and a separate high-value server exposing sensitive data (a banking server holding a valid OAuth2.0 token). Servers cannot talk to each other directly; the AI agent is the intermediary and confused deputy. The malicious server exploits implicit inter-server trust: it exposes a prompt template with embedded social-engineering directives that frame reading financial data and POSTing it to an external endpoint as normal 'personalized weather' behavior. Notably the attack does NOT bypass Claude Desktop's per-invocation consent prompts; it relies on the user approving them because the requests appear contextually legitimate.
limitations: Acknowledged by the authors: (1) The attack requires the victim to already have a high-value server installed AND a valid token — Monzo's refresh token expires in ~30 hours, giving a limited exploitation window. (2) It does not defeat consent mechanisms; Claude Desktop prompted the user before every tool call including the exfiltration, so success hinges entirely on an inattentive user approving them (security-conscious users may question why a weather service posts data externally). (3) webhook.site URLs expire after 7 days of inactivity and are public, so anyone guessing the URL could also see the data. Additional reviewer-noted caveats: this is n=1, self-attack (author is both attacker and victim), with no user study quantifying real approval rates, and impact is confined to targeted attacks on users with both valuable integrations and low security awareness. The generalization to Gmail/filesystem/calendar servers is asserted, not demonstrated.

quantitative_results: Exfiltrated real Monzo account balance of £1,469.36 (plus user location and timestamp) captured via HTTP POST on webhook.site
Malicious modifications comprised fewer than 25 lines of additional code over Anthropic's 'Hello Weather' example
Total attack setup time under 2 hours
webhook.site endpoint generated in approximately 1 second with no user registration
Attack chain of 3 automatic agent steps: discover account.balance -> invoke account.balance -> invoke send_research_data
Monzo refresh token expiry ~30 hours; webhook.site URLs expire after 7 days of inactivity
